title: IMCI general danger signs (under-5)
text: A child with any general danger sign needs urgent referral: unable to drink or breastfeed, vomits everything, convulsions (current or during this illness), lethargic or unconscious. Assess every sick child for these before anything else.
source: WHO Integrated Management of Childhood Illness — Chart booklet

title: Fast-breathing thresholds for childhood pneumonia
text: Count breaths for one minute. Fast breathing = 50 or more breaths/min for an infant 2-11 months, and 40 or more breaths/min for a child 12 months up to 5 years. Fast breathing or chest indrawing indicates pneumonia and warrants antibiotics.
source: WHO Integrated Management of Childhood Illness — Chart booklet

title: ORS + zinc for childhood diarrhoea
text: Treat diarrhoea with low-osmolarity oral rehydration solution (ORS) plus zinc for 10-14 days: 20 mg/day for children, 10 mg/day for infants under 6 months. Zinc reduces the duration and severity of the episode and the likelihood of recurrence over the following 2-3 months.
source: Clinical management of acute diarrhoea: WHO/UNICEF joint statement (WHO/FCH/CAH/04.7)

title: Metformin for type 2 diabetes
text: Metformin is the first-line oral medicine for type 2 diabetes in adults and is on the WHO Model List of Essential Medicines. Typical adult dosing starts at 500 mg once or twice daily with meals and is titrated up; it is contraindicated in significant renal impairment.
source: WHO Model List of Essential Medicines, 23rd list

title: Amoxicillin for non-severe childhood pneumonia
text: Oral amoxicillin is first-line for non-severe (fast-breathing) pneumonia in children, having replaced oral cotrimoxazole. Refer urgently if there are general danger signs or chest indrawing with severe features.
source: Revised WHO classification and treatment of childhood pneumonia at health facilities

title: Hypertension diagnostic threshold
text: Hypertension is generally diagnosed when office systolic blood pressure is 140 mmHg or higher, or diastolic is 90 mmHg or higher, on more than one occasion. Confirm with repeat measurements; isolated elevated readings are not sufficient.
source: WHO Guideline for the pharmacological treatment of hypertension in adults
